source: This is a 28-year-old healthy woman, no significant past medical history, came in after she cut her left forearm on a broken glass about an hour ago while doing dishes. No other injuries, didn't hit her head, no numbness or tingling in the hand, moving all fingers fine. On exam there's a 4-centimeter linear laceration to the volar aspect of the left forearm, edges are clean, no foreign body visible, no active bleeding, distal neurovascular intact — radial pulse 2+, sensation intact, full range of motion of the wrist and fingers, tendons look intact on exam. I irrigated it with saline, anesthetized with lidocaine, and closed it with five 4-0 nylon sutures. Tolerated the procedure well. Tetanus is up to date per the patient. Discharging home with wound care instructions, suture removal in 10 days.
reference:
HPI: 28F, healthy, presents 1 hour after lacerating her left volar forearm on broken glass while washing dishes. Denies other injury, head strike, or hand numbness/tingling; moving all fingers.

Exam: 4 cm clean linear laceration, volar left forearm. No foreign body, no active bleeding. Distal neurovascular intact — radial pulse 2+, sensation intact, full ROM wrist/fingers, tendons intact on exam.

Procedure: Saline irrigation, lidocaine local anesthesia, closure with 5x 4-0 nylon sutures. Tolerated well.

Assessment/Plan: Uncomplicated forearm laceration, repaired. Tetanus up to date per patient. Discharge with wound-care instructions; suture removal in 10 days.
